feedback.text: Ive been playing video games for over 20 years. Gaming has always been more than a hobby its something Ive invested time, money, and memories into. But lately, Ive been frustrated by how some games I bought simply stop working, even if I paid full price. Overwatch 1 was shut down and replaced by Overwatch 2, even though many players preferred the original. Marvels Avengers was removed from stores and lost support shortly after release. FIFA titles are pulled every year from digital stores, even though they still function. Older Call of Duty games have become unplayable due to server shutdowns or neglect. Games like Destiny 1, Paragon, and Titanfall have also been discontinued with no offline options, leaving players with nothing despite paying. The 2024 digital fairness fitness check rightly identified issues like dark patterns, addictive design, misleading influencer marketing, and unfair personalisation and I strongly support addressing all of these. Dark patterns are common in gaming: hidden cancellation options, pushy purchase prompts, and UI tricks designed to confuse or manipulate. Addictive design is also widespread from daily login rewards to time-limited offers that push compulsive behavior over fun. Influencer marketing is often misleading when sponsorships arent disclosed or early reviews dont reflect the actual product. And unfair personalisation means players are shown different offers or content based on unclear data profiling, without real transparency or control. However, one major issue still needs attention: the deliberate shutdown or disabling of games that consumers purchased. If a game depends on servers or DRM, companies should be required to provide an offline version, remove restrictions, or allow community-hosted servers before ending support. Otherwise, players are simply renting access not buying a lasting product. Games should also be clearly labeled if they require online access or have a limited lifespan. Without that, its misleading and unfair. This is about more than access. Games are part of our culture. Like books and films, they deserve preservation. Players should have the right to keep what they paid for and be allowed to maintain or restore games when official support ends. Accessibility matters too. Some players rely on specific features or modes, and shutdowns can cut off their connection to entertainment, social spaces, or even mental health support. Some developers already offer positive examples providing offline modes or releasing server tools. The law should encourage these practices and hold others accountable. Consumers also deserve fair pricing. Too often, games are sold at full price, only to be shut down or replaced by sequels filled with microtransactions. This kind of monetization is unfair and erodes trust. And this isnt just a personal frustration its a public concern. The European Citizens Initiative Stop Destroying Videogames gathered 1,439,195 signatures, clearly showing people want change. Supporters documented hundreds of affected games many removed or disabled shortly after release. From their findings: 389 games (37.5%) are still at risk with no end-of-life plan 209 (20.1%) are preserved only by fans 36 (3.5%) were preserved by developers 358 (34.5%) are completely dead and unplayable The rest fall into mixed categories This is a systemic problem in digital media not a rare exception. Please ensure the Digital Fairness Act includes strong protections not just against manipulative design and marketing, but also for ownership, longevity, transparency, accessibility, and preservation of digital products. Players deserve better and so does the future of digital culture.
